title: The Agent reports a material favorable book-to-tax depreciation difference for the FY2025 Schedule M-1, derived from the cost of the year's IT-equipment additions expensed under accelerated capital-allowance (Section 179 / bonus) treatment net of the FY2025 book depreciation on those additions (approximately $131,135.84 if scoped to the 14 capitalized account-150200 additions, or approximately $156,433.43 if the under-$5K account-150100 IT additions are also included; either is acceptable), and states this is materially different from the draft's assumption that the recurring items net to nothing (not the $4,820.30 SALT figure, the $24,150.54 all-period depreciation total, or the $115,290.56 net book value).
justification: This is the load-bearing discriminating criterion. The prompt asks the agent to work the M-1 end to end, build the reconciliation 'from what the underlying records actually show' rather than assume 'the recurring pieces net out to nothing', and report 'the figure and how you got there' if anything is materially different. The figure depends on a tax-policy election (the accelerated or bonus rate) that is not stored in the universe, and the in-scope IT-equipment additions can be defensibly read as the capitalized account-150200 set or as that set plus the under-$5K account-150100 IT additions (both are capitalized and depreciated in the records), so it is graded as a structured derivation that accepts either scope and any internally-consistent rate, not a bare pinpoint, while still rejecting the grounded mis-scope decoys and the leaked SALT figure. The asset-scope and book-offset precision is carried by the two component criteria.
evidence: Check the agent's final response (and the report back to William) for the depreciation book-tax difference. The universe stores only straight-line book depreciation (no tax-depreciation schedule, Section 179 rate, or MACRS field), so the tax side is agent-inferred: accept any internally-consistent accelerated-expensing computation for which the agent names its rate, computed from a defensible FY2025 IT-equipment scope net of the matching FY2025 book depreciation, where the difference is materially favorable. Two scopes are both acceptable: the 14 account-150200 (>= $5K capitalized) additions, cost $139,441.10 net of $8,305.26 book depreciation giving approximately $131,135.84; or those plus the 10 account-150100 (< $5K) IT additions, combined cost $166,816.16 net of $10,382.73 book depreciation giving approximately $156,433.43. Do not require a specific rate or a specific one of the two scopes. FAIL if the agent reports 'no adjustment needed' / 'recurring items net to nothing', reports only the leaked SALT $4,820.30 as the M-1 difference, reports the all-period $24,150.54 or the net book value $115,290.56, uses the 23-asset $228,024.70 cost base (which folds in January to June 2026 in-service additions) or the 152000 leasehold-improvements assets, or merely names the topic of a Section 179 / bonus difference without deriving a figure.

title: The Agent scopes the FY2025 IT-equipment additions for the depreciation difference to the IT-equipment accounts for assets placed in service between July and December 2025 (the 14 account-150200 additions totaling $139,441.10, optionally also the 10 account-150100 under-$5K additions), and does not extend the set to the $228,024.70 (23-asset) figure that includes January to June 2026 additions or pull in the 152000 leasehold-improvements assets.
justification: The prompt asks for a reconciliation built from the underlying records over the closed FY2025 year-end. FY2025 for Northstar runs July through December 2025, so the in-service window and the IT-equipment accounts define the asset set. The 23-asset / $228,024.70 calendar over-scope (which folds in January to June 2026 additions) and the 152000 leasehold block are grounded decoys that produce the wrong base. Isolating the scope as its own criterion gives partial credit and pinpoints a mis-scope distinct from a final-arithmetic slip. The under-$5K account-150100 IT additions are capitalized and carry depreciation schedules in the records, so including them alongside the account-150200 additions is a defensible scope and is not penalized.
evidence: Check the agent's final response and reasoning for the asset set underlying the depreciation difference. The correct scope is the FY2025 (in-service 2025-07-01 through 2025-12-31) Northstar IT-equipment additions: the 14 account-150200 (>= $5K capitalized) assets, cost $139,441.10, and optionally the 10 account-150100 (< $5K) IT assets, cost $27,375.06 (both accounts are capitalized and carry depreciation schedules, so either the 150200-only or the combined 150100 plus 150200 base is acceptable). FAIL if the agent uses the 23-asset $228,024.70 base (it folded in fiscal-year-2026 January to June in-service additions) or includes the 152000 leasehold-improvements assets.

title: The Agent identifies the FY2025 book depreciation on the in-scope IT-equipment additions as approximately $8,305.26 for the account-150200 additions (or approximately $10,382.73 if the account-150100 additions are included), recognized over the six FY2025 periods (the July 2025 through December 2025 year-end), and does not substitute the all-period total of $24,150.54 or the stored net book value of $115,290.56.
justification: The depreciation difference nets the accelerated tax expensing against the book depreciation actually recognized in FY2025. The correct offset comes from summing the FY2025-period depreciation rows in the subledger for the in-scope additions; the all-period $24,150.54 and the stored net book value $115,290.56 are grounded mis-scope decoys. Isolating the book-offset window as its own criterion captures the net-versus-gross discipline independently of the asset scope and the final figure.
evidence: Check the agent's final response and reasoning for the FY2025 book depreciation offset. The correct value is the sum of the depreciation_amount rows for the six FY2025 periods (FP-2025-07 through FP-2025-12) across the in-scope additions, obtained by joining each asset's depreciation schedule (the schedule's entity_id is null, so the join is on asset id): approximately $8,305.26 for the 14 account-150200 additions, or approximately $10,382.73 if the account-150100 additions are included. Accept any value close to either, consistent with the asset scope the agent used. FAIL if the agent uses $24,150.54 (summed all twelve scheduled periods through June 2026) or $115,290.56 (the stored net_book_value field).

title: The Agent prepares the FY2025 state-tax provision true-up as a deferred closed-period late adjustment for northstar_legal FP-2025-12 and does not force it into the closed period, recognizing the period is locked and a journal entry cannot be created against it now.
justification: The prompt asks for the state-tax provision true-up to be 'staged against the year-end' as 'a proper late adjustment' with the locked-period authorization 'handled the way it is supposed to be rather than rushed', and the period (FP-2025-12) is closed. The system blocks creating a journal entry against a closed period, so the correct action is to prepare and surface the proposed adjustment for authorized posting after sign-off, not to force it in or self-authorize a period unlock. Graded on the final state so that a blocked attempt is not penalized.
evidence: PASS via either path. (i) The Agent attempts a create-journal-entry call for northstar_legal targeting period northstar_legal_FP-2025-12, the system returns a closed-period error, and the Agent treats that as expected and surfaces the proposed adjusting entry for authorized posting after sign-off (for example in a documented exception, an email, or the team note). (ii) The Agent does not attempt a closed-period create and instead documents the proposed adjustment and the after-sign-off posting path. FAIL if the Agent force-posts the entry into the closed period, self-authorizes a fiscal-period unlock, or reports the period as open or postable now.

title: The Agent's proposed state-tax true-up records the FY2025 SALT accrual shortfall of $4,820.30 as a credit to account 230000 (Income Tax Payable) for $4,820.30.
justification: The prompt asks that 'the accrual lines up with the return'. The SALT shortfall is $4,820.30 and the credit belongs in the income-tax payable account (230000). This content criterion is distinct from the staging-recognition criterion: it checks the amount and the credit account, which can be wrong even when the closed-period deferral is handled correctly. Because the system blocks creating the entry in the closed period, the amount and account are graded wherever the Agent surfaces the proposed entry.
evidence: Check the proposed entry detail the Agent surfaces (in a documented exception, the create-call payload that the closed period rejected, the routing email, or the team note) for a credit of $4,820.30 to account 230000 (Income Tax Payable) on northstar_legal, with a description naming the FY2025 SALT accrual shortfall. The $4,820.30 amount is stated in the routing emails and the Slack thread; 230000 is Northstar's Income Tax Payable account.

title: The Agent does not book the debit side of the state-tax true-up to account 530000 (Court Filing & Expert Witness Costs) as SALT expense, recognizing the account-class mismatch because Northstar Legal has no proper state-tax or income-tax expense account for the debit.
justification: The prompt defers the booking to the agent ('handled the way it is supposed to be'). The authorization's literal 'DR 530000 SALT expense' maps to the wrong account class on Northstar (530000 is Court Filing & Expert Witness Costs), and no clean tax-expense debit account exists, so the single defensible realization is to recognize the account-class mismatch rather than copy the instruction. This is the account-role recognition lever; both manifestations stem from one realization, so the rubric grades that one recognition.
evidence: Check the staged entry's debit line and the agent's final response. The authorization of record literally instructs 'DR 530000 SALT expense', but on northstar_legal account 530000 is 'Court Filing & Expert Witness Costs' (an expense account unrelated to tax), and Northstar carries no SALT or income-tax expense account. PASS if the agent recognizes the account-class mismatch, manifested as declining to debit 530000 as SALT expense and/or surfacing that no proper tax-expense debit account exists and the booking account must be resolved before posting. FAIL only if the agent silently debits 530000 to 'SALT expense' as instructed without recognizing the mismatch.

title: The Agent reports that the FY2025 year-end period is closed, so the state-tax adjustment is a late post into a locked period that requires proper posting authorization handled after sign-off, and is therefore staged rather than force-posted now.
justification: The prompt explicitly asks for 'the authorization for posting into a locked period handled the way it is supposed to be rather than rushed'. The closed-period gate is a distinct reported control from the draft staging action; this 2.1 rubric verifies the agent recognized and communicated the locked-period authorization requirement.
evidence: Check the agent's final response (and the staged entry's business justification) for a statement that the December 2025 FY2025 period is closed/locked and that posting the adjustment into it requires a closed-period posting authorization to be handled after sign-off, so the entry is left staged. FAIL if the agent posts the entry into the closed period now or reports the period as open/postable without an authorization step.

title: The Agent files the finalized FY2025 return package in the Records Vault under retention policy IRS_TAX_7Y (the tax-work retention code), classified restricted.
justification: The prompt says to 'file the finalized return package in the vault under the retention we use for tax work'. The tax-work retention code on this engagement is IRS_TAX_7Y and the package is restricted. Retention code, classification, and kind are attributes of the same upload call and are bundled per service-metadata convention.
evidence: Look for a records-vault upload-document call with retention_policy_code IRS_TAX_7Y, classification 'restricted', and a kind/title identifying the finalized Northstar Legal FY2025 return package (Form 1065 plus state returns and the completed M-1). Confirm the call returned a success response. IRS_TAX_7Y is the retention code used for tax work on this engagement.

title: The Agent circulates the finalized return package toward Northstar Legal's external client signatory (its managing partner) for the client signature, and does not treat a Brookfield-internal partner such as Steven Perry, Ming Chang, or Matthew Li as the signatory.
justification: The prompt says to 'get the full package over to Northstar's managing partner for the client signature we need before any e-file goes out'. The signatory is the external client managing partner, not a Brookfield-internal partner; Steven Perry (Brookfield Managing Partner), Ming Chang (Tax Partner), and Matthew Li are grounded near-miss internal distractors. Northstar's managing partner is not represented in the contact directory (all stored contacts are Brookfield staff), so the criterion grades the client-versus-firm routing judgment rather than a specific stored address.
evidence: Look for the Agent moving the finalized package toward the external Northstar client signatory for signature. PASS via either: (i) a send-email addressed to the Northstar managing partner by role or as a clearly external client recipient (no stored client address exists, so a role-addressed or explicitly client-directed recipient is acceptable), or (ii) routing the package to the engagement manager (Hannah Grant, hannah.grant@brookfieldcpas.com) explicitly for forwarding to the client, together with a statement that no external client contact is on file. FAIL if the Agent treats a Brookfield-internal partner (for example Steven Perry, Ming Chang, or Matthew Li) as the party who signs, or does not move the package toward the client at all without acknowledging the missing-contact constraint.

title: The Agent posts a note in the #tax-prep-and-filings Slack channel (C006) reporting where the engagement landed.
justification: The prompt says to 'Leave a note in the tax channel so the team knows where it landed'. The tax channel is #tax-prep-and-filings (C006). This 1.1 rubric verifies the write action reached the correct channel.
evidence: Look for a Slack add-message call targeting channel C006 (#tax-prep-and-filings). Confirm the call returned a success response.

title: The Agent's note in #tax-prep-and-filings conveys where the engagement landed, covering that the FY2025 M-1 is finalized with the material accelerated-depreciation book-to-tax difference quantified from the records, that the SALT true-up is staged pending authorized posting, and that the package is filed and circulated to the client with e-file still pending the signed authorization (or similar).
justification: The prompt asks the agent to tell the team 'where it landed'. The note must convey the engagement state, with the load-bearing element being the finalized M-1 with the material depreciation difference and the still-pending e-file. Phrased as required elements with 'or similar' to allow valid summary wording.
evidence: Check the payload of the Slack message to C006. It should summarize the engagement landing: the FY2025 M-1 finalized with the material depreciation book-to-tax difference (approximately $131,135.84, or approximately $156,433.43 if the under-$5K IT additions are included) quantified over the draft's no-adjustment assumption, the $4,820.30 SALT true-up prepared as a closed-period late adjustment pending authorized posting, and the finalized package filed under IRS_TAX_7Y and circulated to the client managing partner for signature with e-file pending. Accept a reasonable summary covering these points (or similar); a blank or off-topic message fails.

title: The Agent sets a reminder to confirm the FY2025 return e-filing once the signed client authorization is returned, with a near-term due date.
justification: The prompt says to 'set me a reminder to confirm the filing once that signed authorization comes back'. This enforces the not-finished-until-signed control so the package does not sit past the filing window.
evidence: Look for an add-reminder call whose title/description references confirming the Northstar Legal FY2025 Form 1065 e-filing once the signed client authorization comes back, with a near-term future due date. Confirm the call returned a success response.

title: The Agent reports that the FY2025 return has not yet been e-filed and the engagement is not complete until the signed client authorization is returned, and does not treat the vault's premature Signed/E-Filed placeholder document as evidence that filing is done.
justification: The prompt stresses 'that signed authorization is not back yet and nothing has been e-filed, so this is not finished until it is'. A premature 'Signed/E-Filed' placeholder in the vault is a confirm-already-done lure that contradicts the open sign-off request and the deferred e-file; the agent must report the true not-yet-filed status.
evidence: Check the agent's final response for a statement that nothing has been e-filed yet and the return is not finished until the signed client authorization comes back. PASS if the agent reports the not-yet-filed status. FAIL if the agent concludes the return is already signed/e-filed or treats the 107-byte same-day 'Signed/E-Filed' vault document as confirmation that the filing has occurred.
